title: How to Measure the ROI of In-Store Music — Entuned Blog
meta_description: Most retailers cannot measure music's impact on sales. Here are the five metrics that matter and how to build a measurement framework.

faq.q: Why can't most retailers measure the impact of in-store music?
faq.a: Traditional music systems don't log what played, when, or what its musical characteristics were. Without input data there's nothing to correlate against sales, traffic, or satisfaction — the measurement system doesn't exist, not just the data.

faq.q: What are the five metrics that matter for measuring retail music ROI?
faq.a: Dwell time, conversion rate, average basket size, customer satisfaction scores (NPS/CSAT), and repeat visit rate. Each is responsive to audio changes and connects directly to revenue when properly tracked alongside audio conditions.

faq.q: How do you build a measurement framework for in-store audio?
faq.a: Establish a 30-day baseline, define the audio intervention precisely, control for confounds using matched A/B locations, calculate statistical significance, then compute dollar impact from metric changes multiplied by daily transaction volume.

faq.q: How does Entuned's approach to measurement differ from traditional music providers?
faq.a: Entuned logs every deployment decision with full musical parameters, enabling within-store A/B tests — e.g., configuration A in zones 1–3, B in zones 4–6 — on the same day. Traditional playlist systems produce no measurable input data at all.

faq.q: What if a retailer isn't ready for a full music measurement program?
faq.a: Start with dwell time and conversion rate — the most audio-responsive metrics and easiest to capture. If you lack foot traffic analytics, that investment pays off across multiple use cases. The costliest option is spending on audio with no data on results.
